raw_case:
Vulvar leiomyoma mimicking Bartholin's gland cyst: A case report
Vulvar leiomyomas are rare benign tumors originating from smooth muscle cells of the vulvar tissue. This report concerns a 31-year-old woman who presented with a painless vulvar mass measuring 5 × 4 cm. The mass was clinically diagnosed as a Bartholin cyst due to its non-tender and soft nature, and as a result the patient underwent wide local excision of the vulvar mass. Histopathology confirmed it to be benign vulvar leiomyoma. This case emphasizes the importance of thorough clinical evaluation and accurate histopathological examination in identifying vulvar masses. This report underscores the diagnostic difficulties associated with rare vulvar lesions and stresses the necessity of a comprehensive approach to their evaluation and treatment.

Highlights

•Vulvar leiomyoma is a rare and benign mass that arises on the vulva.•Vulvar leiomyoma and Bartholin's cyst share similar symptoms, frequently resulting in misdiagnosis.•A wide local excision followed by a histopathological examination is the most reliable approach to diagnosis of vulvar leiomyoma.1

Introduction

Extrauterine leiomyomas, originating from any organ system that contains smooth muscle, are exceedingly rare [1]. Among these, leiomyoma of the vulva is exceptional, accounting for a mere 0.03 % of all gynecological tumors and 0.07 % of all vulvar neoplasms [2]. Vulvar leiomyomas usually affect females of reproductive age [3]. The similarity in symptoms between vulvar leiomyoma and Bartholin's cyst, such as a painless lump and swelling of the area, often leads to misdiagnosis, with the more common preoperative diagnosis being Bartholin's gland cyst [4]. Another diagnostic challenge with vulvar masses is the differentiation between benign and malignant types, as most have a similar appearance. Due to the low incidence of the tumor, there are no established evidence-based diagnostic algorithms or published recommendations for treatment, highlighting the need for further research in this area. Excision of vulvar leiomyomas remains the only viable surgical option for a complete cure [5].We present a case that initially appeared as a painless vulvar mass, clinically diagnosed as a Bartholin cyst due to its non-tender and soft nature. However, the subsequent surgical exploration revealed a surprising histopathological diagnosis of vulvar leiomyoma, underscoring the unexpected nature of this case.2

Case Presentation

A 31-year-old woman, para 3, with previous vaginal deliveries, presented with a history of right vulvar swelling for four months. She first noted the mass herself and observed a gradual progression in size over that period. There was no history of discharge, pain, fever, weight loss, or bowel or bladder complaints. She was experiencing discomfort at the perineum, mainly in sitting and walking. She denied any history of sexual trauma, dyspareunia, or other vulvar pathology. She attained menarche at the age of 11 years, and her menstrual cycles were regular with normal flow. During her previous pregnancy one year earlier, she was diagnosed with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). Since then, she had been followed by an infectious disease physician and was on antiviral medication. Past surgical history was notable for lipoma excision on her left side. She was a non-smoker with an unremarkable social and family history.Her body mass index was 28.6 kg/m2. Abdominal examination was normal. External genital examination revealed a solitary swelling measuring 5 × 4 cm in the lower part of the left labia majora at the site of the Bartholin gland. The mass was soft in consistency, non-tender, and freely mobile without abnormalities of the surrounding skin. There was no palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy. A vaginal and speculum exam revealed no abnormalities, and no adnexal mass was detected. A basic blood workup and pelvis ultrasound were performed, and no abnormalities were observed. The differential diagnoses were Bartholin cyst, lipoma, sarcoma, and aggressive angiomyxoma. At this point, it was decided to proceed with wide local excision.The patient underwent wide local excision of the mass without any complications, and the lesion was sent for histopathology. Gross pathology examination revealed a well-circumscribed mass measuring 4 cm in its greatest dimension. The outer surface was smooth and intact. Serial sectioning of the specimen revealed no areas of necrosis or hemorrhage. The specimen was left for overnight fixation in 10 % neutral buffered formalin. Histopathology examination with hematoxylin and eosin staining (H&E) revealed benign mesenchymal tumor of a smooth muscle origin (Fig. 1A). It comprised monotonous spindle cells with indistinct borders arranged in intersecting fascicles. Mitotic figures were rare, with less than 5/10 high power fields (HPF). Only the focal area of the lesion showed myxoid cellular changes (Fig. 1B). The tumor had a strong and diffuse positivity for desmin and h-caldesmon (Fig. 1C), and there was also focal and patchy positivity for smooth muscle actin (SMA) (Fig. 1D), estrogen receptors (ER) (Fig. 1E), and progesterone receptors (PR) (Fig. 1F). Although the tumor exhibited morphological characteristics consistent with a leiomyoma, smooth muscle tumors are rare in these regions. Given the patient's immunodeficiency disorder, in situ hybridization (ISH) for Epstein-Barr encoding RNA (EBER) was performed to rule out Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)-associated smooth muscle tumors (EBV-SMTs). The test result was negative, leading to the final diagnosis of leiomyoma.The patient was discharged home the day following surgery with a follow-up after four weeks. She was discharged on oral analgesia and advice for wound care. She recovered uneventfully and completely and went back to her normal daily activity.3

Discussion

Leiomyomas are benign soft-tissue neoplasms of mesenchymal origin, typically found in the uterus [6]. Although rare, vulvar leiomyomas are most commonly located in the labia majora and clitoris [7]. Despite their rarity, understanding their morphological and epidemiological characteristics remains a challenge [8]. While the causes of vulvar leiomyomas are not fully understood, it is believed that estrogens and progesterone may contribute to tumor proliferation, as fibroids rarely appear before menarche and often resolve after menopause [6]. Additionally, during pregnancy, it is unlikely for the tumor to undergo changes in growth or invasion, as observed by Tavassoli and Norris [9].The differential diagnosis of a vulvar mass includes soft-tissue sarcoma, Bartholin cyst, fibroma, lymphangioma, and neurogenic tumor. According to the literature review, vulvar leiomyomas can range in size from 0.5 to 15 cm [10]. The present case involved a painless mass in the lower part of the left vulvar region, near the Bartholin gland, which grew to 5 × 4 cm over four months, leading to difficulty in sitting and walking.Using magnetic resonance imaging and transvaginal ultrasound, it is possible to diagnose vulvar leiomyoma and distinguish it from leiomyosarcoma cm [2]. The diagnosis of a Bartholin cyst in the case reported here was conclusively made based on the patient's medical history and a thorough clinical examination.Leiomyoma of the vulva should not be assumed to be benign without a histological study. Nielsen et al. found that out of 25 cases of vulvar leiomyomas, 4 were atypical and 5 were sarcomas [11]. This reiterates the need for a histological study, as in the present case. The most reliable way to confirm the diagnosis is through a wide local excision followed by a histopathological examination.Histologically, vulvar leiomyomas closely resemble leiomyomas found in the uterus. Complete removal of both the leiomyomas and their capsule is crucial to minimize the risk of recurrence. Kothandaraman et al. documented a case of vulvar leiomyoma recurrence four years following primary excision [1]. It is imperative to convey to the patient the potential for recurrence and emphasize the necessity of regular, long-term follow-up.The primary constraint of the present report is that no pre-operative radiological imaging was done in this case. The clinical findings and location were consistent with a Bartholin gland cyst, and so the patient's management proceeded to wide local surgery. It was a surprise that histopathology subsequently revealed a vulvar leiomyoma.In conclusion, vulvar leiomyoma tumors are often mistaken for Bartholin's cysts, making diagnosis difficult. The most effective diagnostic procedure is excisional biopsy. Follow-up appointments are essential after treatment.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Female, 31 years old, para 3, with a history of prior vaginal deliveries.
- **Chief Complaint:** Painless swelling in the right vulvar region.
- **History of Present Illness:** Four-month history of progressive right vulvar swelling, noted by the patient herself. Discomfort while sitting and walking reported. No associated pain, discharge, fever, weight loss, or urinary/bowel symptoms.
- **Past Medical History:** Diagnosed with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) one year ago; currently on antiviral medication. Past surgical history includes lipoma excision on the left side.
- **Personal History:** Non-smoker.
- **Family History:** Unremarkable.
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - External genital exam: Soft, non-tender, freely mobile 5 × 4 cm swelling in the lower part of the left labia majora, consistent with the site of the Bartholin gland. Surrounding skin appeared normal. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.
  - Abdominal, vaginal, and speculum examinations were unremarkable.
- **Ancillary Tests:** Basic blood work and pelvic ultrasound showed no abnormalities. Post-surgical histopathology confirmed a benign vulvar leiomyoma with strong positivity for desmin and h-caldesmon and patchy positivity for smooth muscle actin (SMA), estrogen receptors (ER), and progesterone receptors (PR). Negative in situ hybridization (ISH) for Epstein-Barr encoding RNA (EBER) ruled out Epstein-Barr virus-associated smooth muscle tumors (EBV-SMTs).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:**
   - **Bartholin's gland cyst:** Initially suspected based on the lesion's location, soft consistency, mobility, and lack of tenderness. A common diagnosis for vulvar swellings.
   - **Lipoma:** Considered due to the patient's prior history of lipoma, but less likely based on clinical features.
   - **Soft-tissue sarcoma:** Evaluated due to the potential malignant nature of vulvar masses, but there was no rapid growth, necrosis, or irregularity suggesting malignancy. Confirmed benign on histopathology.
   - **Aggressive angiomyxoma:** Included due to its soft-tissue characteristics and vulvar location but ruled out as the pathology showed no myxoid stromal changes characteristic of this diagnosis.

2. **Rationale for Final Diagnosis:**
   - Histopathology revealed a benign mesenchymal tumor of smooth muscle origin with spindle cells and low mitotic activity.
   - Immunohistochemical analysis strongly positive for smooth muscle markers (desmin, h-caldesmon) further supported the diagnosis of leiomyoma.
   - Negative testing for EBV-associated markers excluded rare association with immunodeficiency disorders.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 31-year-old female with three prior vaginal deliveries. Her main issue is a painless swelling on the right vulva that's been growing over four months. She's experiencing discomfort when sitting and walking but no pain, discharge, or systemic symptoms like fever. She has a history of HIV diagnosed a year ago and is on antivirals. Also, she had a lipoma removed from the left side before.

First, I need to think about common vulvar swellings. Bartholin's cyst comes to mind since the swelling is in the area of the Bartholin gland. But wait, the physical exam says the swelling is on the left labia majora, not the right. Wait, the chief complaint says right vulvar region, but the exam found a left labia majora swelling. Maybe a typo? But let's proceed. Bartholin's cysts are usually tender if infected, but this is painless. However, they can sometimes be asymptomatic. But the histopathology after surgery showed a vulvar leiomyoma. So maybe the location was correctly reported.

Leiomyomas are benign smooth muscle tumors. They can occur in the vulva, though less commonly than uterine fibroids. The immunostaining markers (desmin, h-caldesmon, SMA) support smooth muscle origin. Positive ER and PR are also typical for leiomyomas, which are hormone-sensitive. The negative EBER rules out EBV-associated tumors, which is important since she's immunocompromised from HIV. EBV-SMTs are more common in immunocompromised patients, but here it's excluded.

Since the histopathology already confirmed it's a benign vulvar leiomyoma, the diagnosis is clear. Even though she's HIV-positive, which might make us think of infections or other tumors, the pathology here is definitive. The prior lipoma excision might be a red herring, but leiomyomas are different from lipomas. The lack of other symptoms aligns with a benign tumor. So the final diagnosis is vulvar leiomyoma.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a painless, progressively enlarging vulvar mass localized to the Bartholin gland area. The primary differential for vulvar swellings includes Bartholin cyst/abscess, lipoma, leiomyoma, or malignancy. However, the non-tender, mobile nature and absence of inflammation or infection signs (e.g., fever, discharge) reduce suspicion for a Bartholin abscess.  
<step 2> The patient’s HIV status raises concern for immunodeficiency-related neoplasms, such as EBV-associated smooth muscle tumors (EBV-SMTs), which are more common in immunocompromised individuals. However, the histopathology results explicitly rule out EBV-SMTs via negative EBER testing.  
<step 3> Histopathology confirms a benign vulvar leiomyoma, supported by strong positivity for desmin and h-caldesmon (smooth muscle markers) and focal positivity for SMA, ER, and PR. The immunoprofile aligns with hormonally responsive smooth muscle tumors, typical of leiomyomas.  
<step 4> The absence of atypical features (e.g., necrosis, mitotic activity) on histopathology, combined with the benign clinical course, excludes malignancy. The prior lipoma excision is unrelated, as lipomas and leiomyomas are distinct entities.  

### Answer:
Benign vulvar leiomyoma

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a painless, progressively enlarging vulvar mass localized to the Bartholin gland area. The primary differential for vulvar swellings includes Bartholin cyst/abscess, lipoma, leiomyoma, or malignancy. However, the non-tender, mobile nature and absence of inflammation or infection signs (e.g., fever, discharge) reduce suspicion for a Bartholin abscess.

The patient’s HIV status raises concern for immunodeficiency-related neoplasms, such as EBV-associated smooth muscle tumors (EBV-SMTs), which are more common in immunocompromised individuals. However, the histopathology results explicitly rule out EBV-SMTs via negative EBER testing.

Histopathology confirms a benign vulvar leiomyoma, supported by strong positivity for desmin and h-caldesmon (smooth muscle markers) and focal positivity for SMA, ER, and PR. The immunoprofile aligns with hormonally responsive smooth muscle tumors, typical of leiomyomas.

The absence of atypical features (e.g., necrosis, mitotic activity) on histopathology, combined with the benign clinical course, excludes malignancy. The prior lipoma excision is unrelated, as lipomas and leiomyomas are distinct entities.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial differential diagnoses include Bartholin's gland cyst, lipoma, soft-tissue sarcoma, and aggressive angiomyxoma based on clinical features.
Step 2: Bartholin's gland cyst is initially suspected due to lesion location, consistency, mobility, and lack of tenderness.
Step 3: Lipoma is considered given the patient's history but is less likely due to clinical features.
Step 4: Soft-tissue sarcoma is evaluated for malignancy but ruled out due to absence of rapid growth, necrosis, or irregularity; confirmed benign on histopathology.
Step 5: Aggressive angiomyxoma is included in differential due to soft-tissue characteristics and vulvar location but ruled out as pathology showed no myxoid stromal changes.
Step 6: Histopathology reveals a benign mesenchymal tumor of smooth muscle origin with spindle cells and low mitotic activity.
Step 7: Immunohistochemical analysis is strongly positive for smooth muscle markers (desmin, h-caldesmon), supporting the diagnosis of leiomyoma.
Step 8: Testing for EBV-associated markers is negative, excluding rare association with immunodeficiency disorders.
Step 9: Final diagnosis is benign vulvar leiomyoma.
